response.output:
# Customising QuickFIX/J

The core QuickFIX/J module is agnostic to FIX Protocol Versions. At runtime a QuickFIX/J dictionary with supporting implementation packages is required to use type-safe classes.

The specification for a FIX integration is called a "Rules of Engagement". The Rules of Engagement can be customised with the mutual agreement of the respective counter-parties.

The message, component and field implementations can be provided by a specialised build, along with the corresponding QuickFIX/J dictionary for the custom Rules of Engagement. 

The standard distribution of ```quickfixj-core``` can be used with custom artefacts. You need only build artefacts for versions of the Protocol that you use. These can be maintained independently from the QuickFIX/J project, while depending on the QuickFIX/J for the core functionality and tools.

To build custom artefacts it's helpful to understand how QuickFIX/J builds the Field, Component and Message classes from the QuickFIX/J dictionaries and from [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/).

The QuickFIX/J reference implementations for FIX versions FIX4.0 to FIX5.0sp2 and for FIXT1.1 are generated from the QuickFIX dictionaries for the specific version. The dictionaries are located in the ```src/main/resources``` directory of the respective modules of the ```quickfixj-messages``` module. 
Maintaining the FIX4.0 to FIX5.0sp2 builds intentionally provides consistency with the prior QuickFIX/J 2 release in order to ease migration to QuickFIX/J 3.  

The most recent standard is defined as [FIX Latest](https://www.fixtrading.org/online-specification/). The QuickFIX/J reference implementation for FIX Latest is generated from a [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) repository file. 
An implementation or customisation of the FIX Standars derived from the FIX Orchestra repository is known as an "_orchestration_". 
The standard FIX Orchestra repository requires some modification to work well with QuickFIX/J. 
This is done by the ```quickfixj-orchestration``` module. 
The ```quickfixj-orchestration``` module publishes a modified Orchestra artefact which can then be the basis of a custom FIX Latest build using QuickFIX/J .

The complete reference FIX Latest specification results in a very large distribution. 
To use FIX Latest, customisation of the [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) repository is advisable.
Please see [QuickFIX/J Orchestration](./quickfixj-orchestration/readme.md) for details.

## Customisation Scenarios

### **Enable the use of ```BigDecimal``` for FIX Decimal Data Types**

This behaviour is controlled by the ```${generator.decimal}``` build property. It is "false" by default to avoid surprising side effects of incompatible data types.

To enable the use of  ```BigDecimal``` in code generation, set the  ```${generator.decimal}``` property to "true" in [quickfixj-messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) and build the message artefacts.

```
	<properties>
		<generator.decimal>true</generator.decimal>
	</properties>
```
See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Incompatible Data Types**

Some incompatible changes have occurred in the evolution of the FIX protocol. For example see below changes to the type of **OrderQty (38)** :

|FIX Version|Field Name|FIX Datatype|Base Type|QuickFIX/J Datatype|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|4.0|OrderQty|int|int|```int```|
|4.2|OrderQty|Qty|float|```Double``` or ```BigDecimal```|

Only one ```quickfix.Field``` class with the same name may be loaded by the Java classloader so only one version of this Field should be in the classpath. QuickFix/J also verifies the data type using the supplied QuickFIX "Dictionary". 

Code generation using ```BigDecimal``` is incompatible at runtime with ```int``` for **OrderQty**. In this case, ```double``` is compatible with ```int``` at run time due to [widening primitive conversion](http://titanium.cs.berkeley.edu/doc/java-langspec-1.0/5.doc.html). 

Runtime incompatibilities can be resolved by:
* Amending the QuickFIX Dictionary to coerce the code generation and/or validation
* Changing the ordering of code generation and/or overwrite behaviour of code generation
* Omitting incompatible versions from your customised build
* Building artefacts independently for the conflicting versions and ensuring they are not used them in the same runtime

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Customising the FIX Protocol for specialised Rules of Engagement**

A Rules of Engagement can include customisation Messages, Components and Fields, including User Defined elements.

It is not necessary to maintain a fork of the entire QuickFIX/J project to provide customised QuickFIX Dictionaries and to 
generate type-safe libraries that are interoperable with QuickFIX/J. 

[FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) is intended for customisation to produce machine-readable Rules of Engagement.

Consider creating a new project (or projects) to build the Messages, Components and Fields as needed for your specific Rules of Engagement. 

Edit the QuickFIX Dictionary or FIX Orchestra Repository (Orchestration) as required and 
build the Messages, Components and Fields packages using the tools provided by the QuickFIX/J projects. 

QuickFIX/J Dictionaries, FIX Orchestra Orchestrations and/or documents can also be generated. 

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Managing incompatibility with Prior Versions of QuickFIX/J**

From QuickFIX/J 3.0.0 the code generation for ```quickfix.Field``` prefers the FIX Orchestra Standard. This results in incompatible changes to the names of constants. 

For example : ```SettlType.REGULAR_FX_SPOT_SETTLEMENT``` becomes ```SettlType.REGULAR```. 

The required code changes may be trivial in most cases, but changes are elective. 
The following describes how to use ```quickfixj-core``` from QuickFIX/J  3 without needing to implement code changes:
* build the required Message artefacts without the FIX Latest code generation. The Fields will then be generated only from legacy FIX Protocol Versions as they were prior to QuickFIX/J 3.0.0 - **or**
* if you want to use Messages, Components and/or Fields from FIX Latest while preferring legacy constants, 
manipulate the order of code generation and/or the over-write behaviour of code behaviour to prefer earlier versions of FIX. 
For example, generate FIX Latest first and overwrite the generated Field classes by subsequently running code generation for an earlier version.

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

# Maven Project Setup Example

### Minimal Project Structure
```text
.
├── pom.xml
└── src
    └── main
        └── resources
            └── FIX44-custom.xml  <-- Your custom dictionary
```

### Maven Configuration (`pom.xml`)
The setup requires two plugins:

1. **`quickfixj-codegenerator`**: generate Java source code from your XML dictionary:
2. **`build-helper-maven-plugin`**: The code generator outputs files to `target/generated-sources`. By default, Maven
   does not know this directory contains source code to be compiled. This plugin adds it to the build path.

```xml
<properties>
    <quickfixj.version>2.3.2</quickfixj.version> <!-- Pick a version -->
</properties>

<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.quickfixj</groupId>
        <artifactId>quickfixj-core</artifactId>
        <version>${quickfixj.version}</version>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.quickfixj</groupId>
            <artifactId>quickfixj-codegenerator</artifactId>
            <version>3.0.2</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <goals><goal>generate</goal></goals>
                    <configuration>
                        <dictFile>src/main/resources/FIX44-custom.xml</dictFile>
                        <packaging>quickfix.fix44</packaging>
                        <fieldPackage>quickfix.field</fieldPackage>
                    </configuration>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
            <artifactId>build-helper-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            <version>3.5.0</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <goals><goal>add-source</goal></goals>
                    <configuration>
                        <sources>
                            <source>target/generated-sources</source>
                        </sources>
                    </configuration>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>
```

### Customizing Messages - Example
Add your custom fields to the `<fields>` section and reference them in the `<messages>` section of your `FIX44-custom.xml`:

```xml
<fields>
    <field number="5000" name="MyCustomField" type="STRING"/>
</fields>
<messages>
    <message name="NewOrderSingle" msgtype="D" msgcat="app">
        <field name="MyCustomField" required="N"/>
    </message>
</messages>
```

### Building

1. To generate sources only:

```bash
mvn generate-sources
```

The classes will be found in `target/generated-sources`.

2. To compile and package sources:

```bash
mvn package
```

The sources will be packaged into the JAR file.

# Gradle Project Setup Example

You can also use a Gradle project to build custom FIX dictionaries.

### Minimal Project Structure

```text
.
├── build.gradle
├── settings.gradle
└── src
    └── main
        └── resources
            └── FIX44-custom.xml  <-- Your custom dictionary
```

### Gradle Configuration (`build.gradle`)

The setup uses the `quickfixj-codegenerator` within the `buildscript` to generate Java source code from your XML
dictionary. It then registers a task that runs the code generator.

```groovy
buildscript {
   repositories {
      mavenCentral()
   }
   dependencies {
      classpath 'org.quickfixj:quickfixj-codegenerator:3.0.2'
   }
}

plugins {
   id 'java'
}

group = 'org.example'
version = '1.0.0-SNAPSHOT'

def quickfixjVersion = '2.3.2'

repositories {
   mavenCentral()
}

dependencies {
   implementation "org.quickfixj:quickfixj-core:${quickfixjVersion}"
}

def generatedSourcesDir = layout.buildDirectory.dir("generated-sources")

tasks.register("generateQuickfix") {
   def dictFile = file("src/main/resources/FIX44-custom.xml")
   inputs.file dictFile
   outputs.dir generatedSourcesDir

   doLast {
      def outDir = generatedSourcesDir.get().asFile
      outDir.mkdirs()
      def generator = new org.quickfixj.codegenerator.MessageCodeGenerator()
      def task = new org.quickfixj.codegenerator.MessageCodeGenerator.Task()
      task.setName("FIX44-custom")
      task.setSpecification(dictFile)
      task.setMessagePackage("quickfix.fix44")
      task.setFieldPackage("quickfix.field")
      task.setOutputBaseDirectory(outDir)
      generator.generate(task)
   }
}

sourceSets.main.java.srcDir generatedSourcesDir
tasks.named("compileJava").configure {
   dependsOn "generateQuickfix"
}
```

### Building

1. To generate sources only:

```bash
gradle generateQuickfix
```

The classes will be found in `build/generated-sources`.

2. To compile and package sources:

```bash
gradle jar
```

The sources will be packaged into the JAR file.

## QuickFIX/J Feature Inventory (for parity comparison)

Explored `/Users/jiayin/workspace/dev/dev/rust/truefix/thrdpty/quickfixj` (QFJ 3.x — a vendored git repo, git-ignored by the parent project). The codebase is a multi-module Maven build. Below is a comprehensive feature inventory organized by the requested categories.

### 1. Top-Level Module Structure
Modules: `quickfixj-all` (uberjar), `quickfixj-base` (version-agnostic core: Field/Message/Dictionary), `quickfixj-core` (engine: Session/transport/store/log), `quickfixj-dictgenerator` (codegen), `quickfixj-orchestration` (FIX Orchestra → dictionary for FIX Latest), `quickfixj-messages` (typed message libs per FIX version), `quickfixj-examples`, `quickfixj-distribution`, `quickfixj-perf-test`, `quickfixj-stress-test`. Plus `docs/`, `customising-quickfixj.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `pom.xml`, `mvnw`.

### 2. quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/ — ~73 top-level classes
Key interfaces/classes: `Connector`, `Acceptor`, `Initiator`, `Application`, `ApplicationExtended` (+ FunctionalAdapter/Adapter variants), `Session`, `SessionState`, `SessionSettings`, `SessionID`, `SessionFactory`/`DefaultSessionFactory`, `SessionSchedule`/`DefaultSessionSchedule`/`DefaultSessionScheduleFactory`, `Responder`, `MessageStore`/`MessageStoreFactory`, `MessageQueue`/`MessageQueueFactory`, `Log`/`LogFactory`/`AbstractLog`, `DataDictionaryProvider`/`DefaultDataDictionaryProvider`, `DefaultMessageFactory`, `MessageCracker`, `MessageSessionUtils`, `RejectLogon`, `SessionStateListener`, `ExecutorFactory`, listener interfaces (`FromAppListener`, `FromAdminListener`, `ToAppListener`).

quickfixj-base (org.quickfixj + quickfix): `Field`, `FieldMap`, `Message`, `MessageComponent`, `Group`, `MessageFactory`, `DataDictionary`, `Dictionary`, `ValidationSettings`, `FixVersions`, `SessionID`, `DayConverter`, `SystemTime`/`SystemTimeSource`, `CharsetSupport`, field type classes (`StringField`, `IntField`, `BooleanField`, `CharField`, `DecimalField`, `DoubleField`, `DateField`, `UtcTimeStampField`, `UtcTimeOnlyField`, `UtcDateOnlyField`, `BytesField`), and `field/converter/` package (Boolean, Char, Decimal, Double, Int, CharArray, UtcTimestamp/TimeOnly/DateOnly converters).

### 3. Session Layer
- **Session.java** is the central state machine (~2900+ lines). Key methods: `logon()`, `logout(reason)`, `next(Message)` (per-message dispatch), `next()` (timer/heartbeat tick), `disconnect(reason, logError)`, `reset()`, `send(message, allowPosDup)`, `isLoggedOn()`, `getExpectedSenderNum()`. Handles resend logic, sequence validation, test requests, logon/logout handshake.
- **SessionState.java** is a `final class` (NOT an enum) holding state as boolean flags: `logonSent`, `logonReceived`, `logoutSent`, `logoutReceived`, plus `logonTimeoutMs` (default 10000), `logoutTimeoutMs` (default 2000), `testRequestCounter`, `lastSentTime`, `lastReceivedTime`, `testRequestDelayMultiplier`. Holds `MessageStore`, `MessageQueue`, `Log`, and separate `ReentrantLock`s for sender/target seq nums. Inner classes: `ResendRange`, `NullLog`. Derived predicates: `isLogonSent`, `isLogonReceived`, `isLogoutSent`, `isLogoutReceived`, logon/logout timeout checks.
- **SessionSettings.java** configures from INI-like files with `[default]` and `[session]` sections. Public session-identity constants only: `BeginString`, `SenderCompID`, `SenderSubID`, `SenderLocationID`, `TargetCompID`, `TargetSubID`, `TargetLocationID`, `SessionQualifier`.

### 4. Transport Layer (MINA-based)
- Public connectors: `SocketAcceptor`, `SocketInitiator`, `ThreadedSocketAcceptor`, `ThreadedSocketInitiator`.
- `quickfix/mina` package: `AbstractIoHandler`, `CompositeIoFilterChainBuilder`, `EventHandlingStrategy` (with `SingleThreadedEventHandlingStrategy` and `ThreadPerSessionEventHandlingStrategy` for single-vs-thread-per-session message dispatch), `IoSessionResponder`, `NetworkingOptions`, `ProtocolFactory`, `QueueTracker`/`QueueTrackers`/`WatermarkTracker` (backpressure/queue management), `SessionConnector`, `CriticalProtocolCodecException`.
- `mina/acceptor`: `AbstractSocketAcceptor`, `AcceptorIoHandler`, `AcceptorSessionProvider`, **`DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider`** (dynamic session creation on incoming logon).
- `mina/initiator`: `AbstractSocketInitiator`, `InitiatorIoHandler`, **`InitiatorProxyIoHandler`** (proxy support), `IoSessionInitiator` (manages reconnect interval array + multiple connect hosts/ports), `ConnectException`.
- `mina/message`: `FIXMessageDecoder`, `FIXMessageEncoder`, `FIXProtocolCodecFactory`, `PatternMatcher`.
- `mina/ssl`: `AcceptorSslFilter`, `InitiatorSslFilter`, `SSLConfig`, `SSLContextFactory`, `SSLSupport`, `X509TrustManagerWrapper`.

### 5. Message Store Layer
`MessageStore` interface + factories: `MemoryStore`, `FileStore` (+ `FileStoreSync`, `FileStoreMaxCachedMsgs`), `CachedFileStore` (in-memory cache over file), `JdbcStore` (DB-backed, with `JdbcUtil`, `JdbcSetting`, configurable table names + connection pool), `SleepycatStore` (Berkeley DB / Oracle JE), `NoopStore` (stateless/no persistence). Plus `MessageQueue` abstraction with `InMemoryMessageQueue` and `BoundInMemoryMessageQueue` (bounded queue for backpressure) and their factories.

### 6. Log Layer
`Log` interface + `AbstractLog` base. Implementations: `ScreenLog` (stdout/stderr), `FileLog` (separate event/incoming/outgoing files), `JdbcLog` (DB tables, configurable), `SLF4JLog` (delegates to SLF4J with per-category routing: event/error/incoming/outgoing categories), `CompositeLog` (multiplex to multiple logs). Plus `LocationAwareLogFactory`, `CompositeLogFactory`, `LogUtil`.

### 7. Dictionary / Message Model
- `DataDictionary` (in base): parses FIX XML dictionaries, validates fields/groups/components, msg-type/tag lookups, field-type checking, required-field validation, custom/UDF support.
- `Message` (base): FIX message with header/body/trailer `FieldMap`s, group iteration, parsing/serialization, header field handling, reverse routing.
- `Field`/`FieldMap`/`Group`/`MessageComponent`: typed field hierarchy, ordered field storage, repeating groups, components.
- `MessageCracker`: visitor base class auto-generating `onMessage(...)` overloads per typed message (used by Application callbacks).
- `MessageFactory`/`DefaultMessageFactory`: creates typed messages by BeginString + MsgType, version-aware.
- `DataDictionaryProvider`/`DefaultDataDictionaryProvider`: per-transport/per-app dictionary selection (needed for FIXT 1.1 session/app split).
- `ValidationSettings`, `MessageUtils`, `MessageSessionUtils`.

### 8. Codegen / Typed Messages / Versions
- `quickfixj-dictgenerator` module: `Generator`, `Message`, `Field`, `Component`, `ComponentComponent`, `ComponentField`, `Enum`, `MsgType`, `MsgTypeField`, `MsgTypeComponent`, `Repository` — generates typed Field/Component/Message Java classes from FIX XML dictionaries.
- `quickfixj-codegenerator` Maven plugin (and Gradle usage documented in `customising-quickfixj.md`) — `MessageCodeGenerator` with `Task` (dictFile, messagePackage, fieldPackage, outputBaseDirectory).
- Supported FIX versions (each a `quickfixj-messages-*` module): **FIX 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.0sp1, 5.0sp2, FIX Latest, FIXT 1.1**, plus `quickfixj-messages-all` (aggregate).
- FIX 4.0–5.0sp2 generated from QuickFIX XML dictionaries; **FIX Latest generated from a FIX Orchestra repository** via `quickfixj-orchestration` module. `generator.decimal` build property toggles `BigDecimal` vs `double` for Qty/decimal fields. Custom Rules-of-Engagement supported by editing dictionaries/orchestration.

### 9. Acceptance Test Definitions
Located at `quickfixj-core/src/test/resources/quickfix/test/acceptance/definitions/` — **481 `.def` scenario files**, organized by version subdirs (`fix40`, `fix41`, `fix42`, `fix43`, `fix44`, `fix50`, `fixLatest`, plus `future/server/fix43`). Scenario categories: `server/` (core protocol: logon/logout, seqnum gaps, resend, reject, garbled msgs, comp-id checks, test requests, session reset — e.g. `1a_ValidLogon`, `2b_MsgSeqNumTooHigh`, `14a_BadField`, `20_SimultaneousResendRequest`, `ReverseRoute`, `SessionReset`), `lastMsgSeqNumProcessed/`, `nextExpectedMsgSeqNum/`, `rejectGarbledMessages/`, `resendRequestChunkSize/` (chunked resend, gap-fill vs resend), `timestamps/` (higher-resolution timestamps), `validateChecksum/`. Java harness under `src/test/java/quickfix/test/acceptance/` (+ `resynch/`).

### 10. Config Keys (SessionSettings)
Config keys are NOT centralized — they live as `SETTING_*` string constants across classes. Comprehensive set found:
- **Session (Session.java)**: `HeartBtInt`, `CheckLatency`, `CheckCompID`, `MaxLatency`, `TestRequestDelayMultiplier`, `HeartBeatTimeoutMultiplier`, `NonStopSession`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `TimeZone`, `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `Weekdays`, `UseDataDictionary`, `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateUnorderedGroupFields`, `FirstFieldInGroupIsDelimiter`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `ValidateIncomingMessage`, `LogonTimeout`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTag`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ValidateSequenceNumbers`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetOnError`, `DisconnectOnError`, `TimeStampPrecision`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ResetOnLogon`, `Description`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `SendRedundantResendRequests`, `PersistMessages`, `ClosedResendInterval`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `DefaultApplVerID`, `DisableHeartBeatCheck`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`, `RejectGarbledMessage`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `RejectMessageOnUnhandledException`, `RequiresOrigSendingTime`, `ForceResendWhenCorruptedStore`, `AllowedRemoteAddresses`, `LogMessageWhenSessionNotFound`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `MaxScheduledWriteRequests`, `ValidateChecksum`, `AllowPosDup`.
- **SessionFactory**: `ConnectionType` (initiator/acceptor), `ContinueInitializationOnError`.
- **Acceptor**: `SocketAcceptProtocol`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `SocketAcceptAddress`, `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Initiator**: `ReconnectInterval`, `SocketConnectProtocol`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketConnectTimeout`, `SocketLocalHost`, `SocketLocalPort`, `ProxyType/ProxyVersion/ProxyHost/ProxyPort/ProxyUser/ProxyPassword/ProxyDomain/ProxyWorkstation`, `DynamicSession`.
- **NetworkingOptions**: `SocketKeepAlive`, `SocketOobInline`, `SocketReceiveBufferSize`, `SocketReuseAddress`, `SocketSendBufferSize`, `SocketLinger`, `SocketTcpNoDelay`, `SocketTrafficClass`, `SocketSynchronousWrites`, `SocketSynchronousWriteTimeout`.
- **FileStore**: `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `FileStoreMaxCachedMsgs`.
- **FileLog**: `FileLogPath`, `FileIncludeMilliseconds`, `FileIncludeTimeStampForMessages`, `FileLogHeartbeats`.
- **SLF4JLog**: `SLF4JLogEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogErrorEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogIncomingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogOutgoingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogPrependSessionID`, `SLF4JLogHeartbeats`.
- **JdbcSetting**: `JdbcDriver`, `JdbcURL`, `JdbcUser`, `JdbcPassword`, `JdbcStoreMessagesTableName`, `JdbcStoreSessionsTableName`, `JdbcDataSourceName`, `JndiContextFactory`, `JndiProviderURL`, `JdbcLogHeartBeats`, `JdbcLogOutgoingTable`, `JdbcLogIncomingTable`, `JdbcLogEventTable`, `JdbcSessionIdDefaultPropertyValue`, plus pool sizing (`JdbcMaxActiveConnection`, `JdbcMinIdleConnection`, `JdbcMaxConnectionLifeTime`, `JdbcConnectionTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionIdleTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionKeepaliveTime`, `JdbcConnectionTestQuery`).
- **SSL**: `SSLSupport.SETTING_USE_SSL` (`UseSSL`) plus keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol settings in `SSLSupport`/`SSLConfig` (exact key strings not fully enumerated — see gap below).

### 11. Examples (quickfixj-examples)
- **executor**: minimal acceptor — `Executor` + `Application` + `MarketDataProvider`; auto-fills any incoming NewOrderSingle with an ExecutionReport (sells to any buy).
- **banzai**: Swing-GUI order-entry **initiator** client — `Banzai`/`BanzaiApplication` + UI (`BanzaiFrame`, `OrderEntryPanel`, `OrderTableModel`, `ExecutionTableModel`), supports New/Cancel/Replace, tracks orders/executions, `OrderSide`/`OrderType`/`OrderTIF` enums.
- **ordermatch**: a simple in-memory **matching engine** — `Application`, `Main`, `Market`, `Order`, `OrderMatcher`, `IdGenerator`; matches orders between multiple sessions.

### 12. SSL/TLS Support
Dedicated `mina/ssl` package: `SSLSupport` (config keys), `SSLConfig` (holds keystore/truststore/protocols/ciphers), `SSLContextFactory` (builds `SSLContext`), `InitiatorSslFilter` + `AcceptorSslFilter` (MINA SSL filters for each direction), `X509TrustManagerWrapper` (custom trust manager). Enabled per-session via `UseSSL=true`; acceptor groups sessions by SSL config + bind address in `AcceptorSocketDescriptor`.

### 13. Scheduling (Session Schedule)
- `SessionSchedule` interface: `isSessionTime()`, `isSameSession(time1, time2)`, `isNonStopSession()`.
- `DefaultSessionSchedule` supports **four modes**: non-stop (`NonStopSession=true`), daily (`StartTime`/`EndTime` only), weekly (`StartDay`/`EndDay` + times), and **multi-weekday** (`Weekdays=Sun,Mon,...` comma list parsed via `DayConverter`). Uses `TimeEndPoint`/`TimeInterval` inner classes with day-of-week + time + TimeZone. `DefaultSessionScheduleFactory` builds from settings. Settings: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `NonStopSession`.

### 14. Reconnect, Dynamic Sessions, Proxy
- **Reconnect**: `ReconnectInterval` accepts a comma-separated list → `int[]` of escalating intervals; `IoSessionInitiator` cycles through `reconnectIntervalInMillis[]` (last value reused) and through multiple `SocketConnectHost`/`SocketConnectPort` endpoints on failure. `SocketConnectTimeout` (default 60s) for connect timeout.
- **Dynamic sessions (acceptor)**: `DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider` + `AcceptorSessionProvider` create `Session` objects on demand for incoming logons not pre-defined; gated by `DynamicSession` setting and `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Proxy support (initiator)**: full HTTP and SOCKS proxy via Apache MINA `proxy` package (`ProtocolFactory.createIoProxyConnector`). Supports SOCKS 4 / 4a / 5 and HTTP 1.0/1.1 with optional Basic auth (`Proxy-Authorization` header), NTLM (`ProxyDomain`/`ProxyWorkstation`). Settings: `ProxyType`, `ProxyVersion`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `ProxyDomain`, `ProxyWorkstation`. Wired through `InitiatorProxyIoHandler` → `IoSessionInitiator`.

- Exact SSL keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol key strings inside `SSLSupport.java`/`SSLConfig.java` were not enumerated (only `SETTING_USE_SSL` confirmed); easy to read if needed.
- Detailed method-level behavior of `Session.next()`/resend state machine read only at signature level (lines 1277/1952/2125) rather than full bodies.
- `quickfixj-perf-test` / `quickfixj-stress-test` module internals not inspected (names imply JMH/perf + stress harnesses).

None of these block the current parity inventory.

- **Session (Session.java)**: `HeartBtInt`, `CheckLatency`, `CheckCompID`, `MaxLatency`, `TestRequestDelayMultiplier`, `HeartBeatTimeoutMultiplier`, `NonStopSession`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `TimeZone`, `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `Weekdays`, `UseDataDictionary`, `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateUnorderedGroupFields`, `FirstFieldInGroupIsDelimiter`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `ValidateIncomingMessage`, `LogonTimeout`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTag`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ValidateSequenceNumbers`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetOnError`, `DisconnectOnError`, `TimeStampPrecision`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ResetOnLogon`, `Description`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `SendRedundantResendRequests`, `PersistMessages`, `ClosedResendInterval`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `DefaultApplVerID`, `DisableHeartBeatCheck`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`, `RejectGarbledMessage`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `RejectMessageOnUnhandledException`, `RequiresOrigSendingTime`, `ForceResendWhenCorruptedStore`, `AllowedRemoteAddresses`, `LogMessageWhenSessionNotFound`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `MaxScheduledWriteRequests`, `ValidateChecksum`, `AllowPosDup`.
- **SessionFactory**: `ConnectionType` (initiator/acceptor), `ContinueInitializationOnError`.
- **Acceptor**: `SocketAcceptProtocol`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `SocketAcceptAddress`, `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Initiator**: `ReconnectInterval`, `SocketConnectProtocol`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketConnectTimeout`, `SocketLocalHost`, `SocketLocalPort`, `ProxyType/ProxyVersion/ProxyHost/ProxyPort/ProxyUser/ProxyPassword/ProxyDomain/ProxyWorkstation`, `DynamicSession`.
- **NetworkingOptions**: `SocketKeepAlive`, `SocketOobInline`, `SocketReceiveBufferSize`, `SocketReuseAddress`, `SocketSendBufferSize`, `SocketLinger`, `SocketTcpNoDelay`, `SocketTrafficClass`, `SocketSynchronousWrites`, `SocketSynchronousWriteTimeout`.
- **FileStore**: `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `FileStoreMaxCachedMsgs`.
- **FileLog**: `FileLogPath`, `FileIncludeMilliseconds`, `FileIncludeTimeStampForMessages`, `FileLogHeartbeats`.
- **SLF4JLog**: `SLF4JLogEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogErrorEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogIncomingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogOutgoingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogPrependSessionID`, `SLF4JLogHeartbeats`.
- **JdbcSetting**: `JdbcDriver`, `JdbcURL`, `JdbcUser`, `JdbcPassword`, `JdbcStoreMessagesTableName`, `JdbcStoreSessionsTableName`, `JdbcDataSourceName`, `JndiContextFactory`, `JndiProviderURL`, `JdbcLogHeartBeats`, `JdbcLogOutgoingTable`, `JdbcLogIncomingTable`, `JdbcLogEventTable`, `JdbcSessionIdDefaultPropertyValue`, plus pool sizing (`JdbcMaxActiveConnection`, `JdbcMinIdleConnection`, `JdbcMaxConnectionLifeTime`, `JdbcConnectionTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionIdleTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionKeepaliveTime`, `JdbcConnectionTestQuery`).
- **SSL**: `SSLSupport.SETTING_USE_SSL` (`UseSSL`) plus keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol settings in `SSLSupport`/`SSLConfig` (exact key strings not fully enumerated — see gap below).

### 11. Examples (quickfixj-examples)
- **executor**: minimal acceptor — `Executor` + `Application` + `MarketDataProvider`; auto-fills any incoming NewOrderSingle with an ExecutionReport (sells to any buy).
- **banzai**: Swing-GUI order-entry **initiator** client — `Banzai`/`BanzaiApplication` + UI (`BanzaiFrame`, `OrderEntryPanel`, `OrderTableModel`, `ExecutionTableModel`), supports New/Cancel/Replace, tracks orders/executions, `OrderSide`/`OrderType`/`OrderTIF` enums.
- **ordermatch**: a simple in-memory **matching engine** — `Application`, `Main`, `Market`, `Order`, `OrderMatcher`, `IdGenerator`; matches orders between multiple sessions.

### 12. SSL/TLS Support
Dedicated `mina/ssl` package: `SSLSupport` (config keys), `SSLConfig` (holds keystore/truststore/protocols/ciphers), `SSLContextFactory` (builds `SSLContext`), `InitiatorSslFilter` + `AcceptorSslFilter` (MINA SSL filters for each direction), `X509TrustManagerWrapper` (custom trust manager). Enabled per-session via `UseSSL=true`; acceptor groups sessions by SSL config + bind address in `AcceptorSocketDescriptor`.

### 13. Scheduling (Session Schedule)
- `SessionSchedule` interface: `isSessionTime()`, `isSameSession(time1, time2)`, `isNonStopSession()`.
- `DefaultSessionSchedule` supports **four modes**: non-stop (`NonStopSession=true`), daily (`StartTime`/`EndTime` only), weekly (`StartDay`/`EndDay` + times), and **multi-weekday** (`Weekdays=Sun,Mon,...` comma list parsed via `DayConverter`). Uses `TimeEndPoint`/`TimeInterval` inner classes with day-of-week + time + TimeZone. `DefaultSessionScheduleFactory` builds from settings. Settings: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `NonStopSession`.

### 14. Reconnect, Dynamic Sessions, Proxy
- **Reconnect**: `ReconnectInterval` accepts a comma-separated list → `int[]` of escalating intervals; `IoSessionInitiator` cycles through `reconnectIntervalInMillis[]` (last value reused) and through multiple `SocketConnectHost`/`SocketConnectPort` endpoints on failure. `SocketConnectTimeout` (default 60s) for connect timeout.
- **Dynamic sessions (acceptor)**: `DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider` + `AcceptorSessionProvider` create `Session` objects on demand for incoming logons not pre-defined; gated by `DynamicSession` setting and `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Proxy support (initiator)**: full HTTP and SOCKS proxy via Apache MINA `proxy` package (`ProtocolFactory.createIoProxyConnector`). Supports SOCKS 4 / 4a / 5 and HTTP 1.0/1.1 with optional Basic auth (`Proxy-Authorization` header), NTLM (`ProxyDomain`/`ProxyWorkstation`). Settings: `ProxyType`, `ProxyVersion`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `ProxyDomain`, `ProxyWorkstation`. Wired through `InitiatorProxyIoHandler` → `IoSessionInitiator`.

### Minor gaps (non-blocking for parity comparison)
- Exact SSL keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol key strings inside `SSLSupport.java`/`SSLConfig.java` were not enumerated (only `SETTING_USE_SSL` confirmed); easy to read if needed.
- Detailed method-level behavior of `Session.next()`/resend state machine read only at signature level (lines 1277/1952/2125) rather than full bodies.
- `quickfixj-perf-test` / `quickfixj-stress-test` module internals not inspected (names imply JMH/perf + stress harnesses).

Evidence base: directory listings, `find` over core/base/mina/test dirs, `grep` of `SETTING_*` constants across Session.java/SessionFactory/Acceptor/Initiator/NetworkingOptions/JdbcSetting/FileStoreFactory/FileLogFactory/SLF4JLogFactory, `SessionSettings.java`/`SessionState.java`/`SessionSchedule.java`/`DefaultSessionSchedule.java` content, and `customising-quickfixj.md`.

Next steps:
If deeper parity detail is needed, the highest-value follow-ups are: (1) read `SSLSupport.java` end-to-end to enumerate the exact SSL keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol key strings; (2) read `Session.java` `next(Message)`/`next()`/`disconnect()` method bodies (lines ~1277–2150, ~2775) to map the exact resend/gap-fill/test-request/sequence-reset state machine; (3) inspect `quickfixj-base/src/main/resources` dictionaries and a sample generated `quickfixj-messages-fix44` class to confirm codegen output shape; (4) skim `quickfixj-perf-test`/`quickfixj-stress-test` modules to characterize load-testing tooling. None of these block the current parity inventory.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

- quickfixj-all
- quickfixj-base (base classes - probably Field, FieldMap, Message, etc.)
- quickfixj-core (core engine - Session, transport, store, log)
- quickfixj-dictgenerator
- quickfixj-distribution
- quickfixj-examples (banzai, executor, ordermatch)
- quickfixj-messages (multiple FIX versions: fix40, fix41, fix42, fix43, fix44, fix50, fix50sp1, fix50sp2, fixlatest, fixt11)
- quickfixj-orchestration
- quickfixj-perf-test
- quickfixj-stress-test

- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.

If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.

- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

messages.content:
<think>The SessionSettings grep only returned 8 results because the constants use a different pattern (no space before =). Let me look at the actual SessionSettings file more carefully - it likely has a large block of constants. Also SessionState grep returned a weird result - it seems SessionState might not be an enum. Let me check the acceptance test location (it's not at src/test/acceptance), and look at the SessionSettings file directly for the constants block.

Let me also note the SSL classes found: AcceptorSslFilter, InitiatorSslFilter, SSLConfig, SSLContextFactory, SSLSupport, X509TrustManagerWrapper. And proxy: InitiatorProxyIoHandler. And dynamic sessions: DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider.

Let me be efficient. I'll:
1. Read the SessionSettings.java constants section (the grep pattern was wrong, constants likely formatted as `public static final String FOO = "Bar"` without the space-* pattern)
2. Find acceptance tests in the right location
3. Check SessionState.java structure

Let me do these in parallel.</think>SessionState isn't a simple enum and acceptance tests aren't at the expected path. Let me fix the SessionSettings grep, find acceptance tests, and check SessionState structure in parallel.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

<think>Excellent progress. Now I have:
- SessionState: boolean flags (logonSent, logonReceived, logoutSent, logoutReceived), logonTimeoutMs, logoutTimeoutMs, testRequestCounter, lastSentTime, lastReceivedTime, testRequestDelayMultiplier, plus ResendRange inner class, NullLog inner class. It's a final class with state flags, NOT an enum.
- 481 .def acceptance test files across versions fix40-fixLatest in categories: server/, lastMsgSeqNumProcessed/, nextExpectedMsgSeqNum/, rejectGarbledMessages/, resendRequestChunkSize/, timestamps/, validateChecksum/
- mina classes found including ssl/, acceptor/, initiator/, message/
- SessionSettings only has 8 public session-id constants. The other config keys must be string literals used inline across classes (not centralized as constants). 

I need to find the config keys used as string literals. Let me grep for common QFJ config keys across the codebase. Common QFJ settings: FileStorePath, ConnectionType, SocketConnectHost, SocketConnectPort, SocketAcceptPort, ReconnectInterval, HeartBtInt, StartTime, StopTime, UseDataDictionary, DataDictionary, BeginString, etc.

Also I need to look at the message versions / codegen, the examples, scheduling (DefaultSessionSchedule), and the Session.java key features.

Let me be efficient with remaining budget. Let me do parallel:
1. Grep config keys across SessionSettings, AbstractSessionConnectorBuilder, Session.java, FileStore, etc. Actually better: grep for getString/setString with string literals, and also look for a settings reference doc.
2. Read DefaultSessionSchedule.java for day/week support
3. List examples structure + look at messages versions / codegen
4. Quick look at SSLConfig/SSLSupport for SSL config keys

response.output:
# Customising QuickFIX/J

The core QuickFIX/J module is agnostic to FIX Protocol Versions. At runtime a QuickFIX/J dictionary with supporting implementation packages is required to use type-safe classes.

The specification for a FIX integration is called a "Rules of Engagement". The Rules of Engagement can be customised with the mutual agreement of the respective counter-parties.

The message, component and field implementations can be provided by a specialised build, along with the corresponding QuickFIX/J dictionary for the custom Rules of Engagement. 

The standard distribution of ```quickfixj-core``` can be used with custom artefacts. You need only build artefacts for versions of the Protocol that you use. These can be maintained independently from the QuickFIX/J project, while depending on the QuickFIX/J for the core functionality and tools.

To build custom artefacts it's helpful to understand how QuickFIX/J builds the Field, Component and Message classes from the QuickFIX/J dictionaries and from [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/).

The QuickFIX/J reference implementations for FIX versions FIX4.0 to FIX5.0sp2 and for FIXT1.1 are generated from the QuickFIX dictionaries for the specific version. The dictionaries are located in the ```src/main/resources``` directory of the respective modules of the ```quickfixj-messages``` module. 
Maintaining the FIX4.0 to FIX5.0sp2 builds intentionally provides consistency with the prior QuickFIX/J 2 release in order to ease migration to QuickFIX/J 3.  

The most recent standard is defined as [FIX Latest](https://www.fixtrading.org/online-specification/). The QuickFIX/J reference implementation for FIX Latest is generated from a [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) repository file. 
An implementation or customisation of the FIX Standars derived from the FIX Orchestra repository is known as an "_orchestration_". 
The standard FIX Orchestra repository requires some modification to work well with QuickFIX/J. 
This is done by the ```quickfixj-orchestration``` module. 
The ```quickfixj-orchestration``` module publishes a modified Orchestra artefact which can then be the basis of a custom FIX Latest build using QuickFIX/J .

The complete reference FIX Latest specification results in a very large distribution. 
To use FIX Latest, customisation of the [FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) repository is advisable.
Please see [QuickFIX/J Orchestration](./quickfixj-orchestration/readme.md) for details.

## Customisation Scenarios

### **Enable the use of ```BigDecimal``` for FIX Decimal Data Types**

This behaviour is controlled by the ```${generator.decimal}``` build property. It is "false" by default to avoid surprising side effects of incompatible data types.

To enable the use of  ```BigDecimal``` in code generation, set the  ```${generator.decimal}``` property to "true" in [quickfixj-messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) and build the message artefacts.

```
	<properties>
		<generator.decimal>true</generator.decimal>
	</properties>
```
See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Incompatible Data Types**

Some incompatible changes have occurred in the evolution of the FIX protocol. For example see below changes to the type of **OrderQty (38)** :

|FIX Version|Field Name|FIX Datatype|Base Type|QuickFIX/J Datatype|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|4.0|OrderQty|int|int|```int```|
|4.2|OrderQty|Qty|float|```Double``` or ```BigDecimal```|

Only one ```quickfix.Field``` class with the same name may be loaded by the Java classloader so only one version of this Field should be in the classpath. QuickFix/J also verifies the data type using the supplied QuickFIX "Dictionary". 

Code generation using ```BigDecimal``` is incompatible at runtime with ```int``` for **OrderQty**. In this case, ```double``` is compatible with ```int``` at run time due to [widening primitive conversion](http://titanium.cs.berkeley.edu/doc/java-langspec-1.0/5.doc.html). 

Runtime incompatibilities can be resolved by:
* Amending the QuickFIX Dictionary to coerce the code generation and/or validation
* Changing the ordering of code generation and/or overwrite behaviour of code generation
* Omitting incompatible versions from your customised build
* Building artefacts independently for the conflicting versions and ensuring they are not used them in the same runtime

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Customising the FIX Protocol for specialised Rules of Engagement**

A Rules of Engagement can include customisation Messages, Components and Fields, including User Defined elements.

It is not necessary to maintain a fork of the entire QuickFIX/J project to provide customised QuickFIX Dictionaries and to 
generate type-safe libraries that are interoperable with QuickFIX/J. 

[FIX Orchestra](https://www.fixtrading.org/standards/fix-orchestra/) is intended for customisation to produce machine-readable Rules of Engagement.

Consider creating a new project (or projects) to build the Messages, Components and Fields as needed for your specific Rules of Engagement. 

Edit the QuickFIX Dictionary or FIX Orchestra Repository (Orchestration) as required and 
build the Messages, Components and Fields packages using the tools provided by the QuickFIX/J projects. 

QuickFIX/J Dictionaries, FIX Orchestra Orchestrations and/or documents can also be generated. 

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

### **Managing incompatibility with Prior Versions of QuickFIX/J**

From QuickFIX/J 3.0.0 the code generation for ```quickfix.Field``` prefers the FIX Orchestra Standard. This results in incompatible changes to the names of constants. 

For example : ```SettlType.REGULAR_FX_SPOT_SETTLEMENT``` becomes ```SettlType.REGULAR```. 

The required code changes may be trivial in most cases, but changes are elective. 
The following describes how to use ```quickfixj-core``` from QuickFIX/J  3 without needing to implement code changes:
* build the required Message artefacts without the FIX Latest code generation. The Fields will then be generated only from legacy FIX Protocol Versions as they were prior to QuickFIX/J 3.0.0 - **or**
* if you want to use Messages, Components and/or Fields from FIX Latest while preferring legacy constants, 
manipulate the order of code generation and/or the over-write behaviour of code behaviour to prefer earlier versions of FIX. 
For example, generate FIX Latest first and overwrite the generated Field classes by subsequently running code generation for an earlier version.

See [QuickFIX/J Messages](./quickfixj-messages/readme.md) for details of the build and recommendation for  **how to implement custom builds.**

# Maven Project Setup Example

### Minimal Project Structure
```text
.
├── pom.xml
└── src
    └── main
        └── resources
            └── FIX44-custom.xml  <-- Your custom dictionary
```

### Maven Configuration (`pom.xml`)
The setup requires two plugins:

1. **`quickfixj-codegenerator`**: generate Java source code from your XML dictionary:
2. **`build-helper-maven-plugin`**: The code generator outputs files to `target/generated-sources`. By default, Maven
   does not know this directory contains source code to be compiled. This plugin adds it to the build path.

```xml
<properties>
    <quickfixj.version>2.3.2</quickfixj.version> <!-- Pick a version -->
</properties>

<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.quickfixj</groupId>
        <artifactId>quickfixj-core</artifactId>
        <version>${quickfixj.version}</version>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.quickfixj</groupId>
            <artifactId>quickfixj-codegenerator</artifactId>
            <version>3.0.2</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <goals><goal>generate</goal></goals>
                    <configuration>
                        <dictFile>src/main/resources/FIX44-custom.xml</dictFile>
                        <packaging>quickfix.fix44</packaging>
                        <fieldPackage>quickfix.field</fieldPackage>
                    </configuration>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
            <artifactId>build-helper-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            <version>3.5.0</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <goals><goal>add-source</goal></goals>
                    <configuration>
                        <sources>
                            <source>target/generated-sources</source>
                        </sources>
                    </configuration>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>
```

### Customizing Messages - Example
Add your custom fields to the `<fields>` section and reference them in the `<messages>` section of your `FIX44-custom.xml`:

```xml
<fields>
    <field number="5000" name="MyCustomField" type="STRING"/>
</fields>
<messages>
    <message name="NewOrderSingle" msgtype="D" msgcat="app">
        <field name="MyCustomField" required="N"/>
    </message>
</messages>
```

### Building

1. To generate sources only:

```bash
mvn generate-sources
```

The classes will be found in `target/generated-sources`.

2. To compile and package sources:

```bash
mvn package
```

The sources will be packaged into the JAR file.

# Gradle Project Setup Example

You can also use a Gradle project to build custom FIX dictionaries.

### Minimal Project Structure

```text
.
├── build.gradle
├── settings.gradle
└── src
    └── main
        └── resources
            └── FIX44-custom.xml  <-- Your custom dictionary
```

### Gradle Configuration (`build.gradle`)

The setup uses the `quickfixj-codegenerator` within the `buildscript` to generate Java source code from your XML
dictionary. It then registers a task that runs the code generator.

```groovy
buildscript {
   repositories {
      mavenCentral()
   }
   dependencies {
      classpath 'org.quickfixj:quickfixj-codegenerator:3.0.2'
   }
}

plugins {
   id 'java'
}

group = 'org.example'
version = '1.0.0-SNAPSHOT'

def quickfixjVersion = '2.3.2'

repositories {
   mavenCentral()
}

dependencies {
   implementation "org.quickfixj:quickfixj-core:${quickfixjVersion}"
}

def generatedSourcesDir = layout.buildDirectory.dir("generated-sources")

tasks.register("generateQuickfix") {
   def dictFile = file("src/main/resources/FIX44-custom.xml")
   inputs.file dictFile
   outputs.dir generatedSourcesDir

   doLast {
      def outDir = generatedSourcesDir.get().asFile
      outDir.mkdirs()
      def generator = new org.quickfixj.codegenerator.MessageCodeGenerator()
      def task = new org.quickfixj.codegenerator.MessageCodeGenerator.Task()
      task.setName("FIX44-custom")
      task.setSpecification(dictFile)
      task.setMessagePackage("quickfix.fix44")
      task.setFieldPackage("quickfix.field")
      task.setOutputBaseDirectory(outDir)
      generator.generate(task)
   }
}

sourceSets.main.java.srcDir generatedSourcesDir
tasks.named("compileJava").configure {
   dependsOn "generateQuickfix"
}
```

### Building

1. To generate sources only:

```bash
gradle generateQuickfix
```

The classes will be found in `build/generated-sources`.

2. To compile and package sources:

```bash
gradle jar
```

The sources will be packaged into the JAR file.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.

- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

args.result:
## QuickFIX/J Feature Inventory (for parity comparison)

Explored `/Users/jiayin/workspace/dev/dev/rust/truefix/thrdpty/quickfixj` (QFJ 3.x — a vendored git repo, git-ignored by the parent project). The codebase is a multi-module Maven build. Below is a comprehensive feature inventory organized by the requested categories.

### 1. Top-Level Module Structure
Modules: `quickfixj-all` (uberjar), `quickfixj-base` (version-agnostic core: Field/Message/Dictionary), `quickfixj-core` (engine: Session/transport/store/log), `quickfixj-dictgenerator` (codegen), `quickfixj-orchestration` (FIX Orchestra → dictionary for FIX Latest), `quickfixj-messages` (typed message libs per FIX version), `quickfixj-examples`, `quickfixj-distribution`, `quickfixj-perf-test`, `quickfixj-stress-test`. Plus `docs/`, `customising-quickfixj.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `pom.xml`, `mvnw`.

### 2. quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/ — ~73 top-level classes
Key interfaces/classes: `Connector`, `Acceptor`, `Initiator`, `Application`, `ApplicationExtended` (+ FunctionalAdapter/Adapter variants), `Session`, `SessionState`, `SessionSettings`, `SessionID`, `SessionFactory`/`DefaultSessionFactory`, `SessionSchedule`/`DefaultSessionSchedule`/`DefaultSessionScheduleFactory`, `Responder`, `MessageStore`/`MessageStoreFactory`, `MessageQueue`/`MessageQueueFactory`, `Log`/`LogFactory`/`AbstractLog`, `DataDictionaryProvider`/`DefaultDataDictionaryProvider`, `DefaultMessageFactory`, `MessageCracker`, `MessageSessionUtils`, `RejectLogon`, `SessionStateListener`, `ExecutorFactory`, listener interfaces (`FromAppListener`, `FromAdminListener`, `ToAppListener`).

quickfixj-base (org.quickfixj + quickfix): `Field`, `FieldMap`, `Message`, `MessageComponent`, `Group`, `MessageFactory`, `DataDictionary`, `Dictionary`, `ValidationSettings`, `FixVersions`, `SessionID`, `DayConverter`, `SystemTime`/`SystemTimeSource`, `CharsetSupport`, field type classes (`StringField`, `IntField`, `BooleanField`, `CharField`, `DecimalField`, `DoubleField`, `DateField`, `UtcTimeStampField`, `UtcTimeOnlyField`, `UtcDateOnlyField`, `BytesField`), and `field/converter/` package (Boolean, Char, Decimal, Double, Int, CharArray, UtcTimestamp/TimeOnly/DateOnly converters).

### 3. Session Layer
- **Session.java** is the central state machine (~2900+ lines). Key methods: `logon()`, `logout(reason)`, `next(Message)` (per-message dispatch), `next()` (timer/heartbeat tick), `disconnect(reason, logError)`, `reset()`, `send(message, allowPosDup)`, `isLoggedOn()`, `getExpectedSenderNum()`. Handles resend logic, sequence validation, test requests, logon/logout handshake.
- **SessionState.java** is a `final class` (NOT an enum) holding state as boolean flags: `logonSent`, `logonReceived`, `logoutSent`, `logoutReceived`, plus `logonTimeoutMs` (default 10000), `logoutTimeoutMs` (default 2000), `testRequestCounter`, `lastSentTime`, `lastReceivedTime`, `testRequestDelayMultiplier`. Holds `MessageStore`, `MessageQueue`, `Log`, and separate `ReentrantLock`s for sender/target seq nums. Inner classes: `ResendRange`, `NullLog`. Derived predicates: `isLogonSent`, `isLogonReceived`, `isLogoutSent`, `isLogoutReceived`, logon/logout timeout checks.
- **SessionSettings.java** configures from INI-like files with `[default]` and `[session]` sections. Public session-identity constants only: `BeginString`, `SenderCompID`, `SenderSubID`, `SenderLocationID`, `TargetCompID`, `TargetSubID`, `TargetLocationID`, `SessionQualifier`.

### 4. Transport Layer (MINA-based)
- Public connectors: `SocketAcceptor`, `SocketInitiator`, `ThreadedSocketAcceptor`, `ThreadedSocketInitiator`.
- `quickfix/mina` package: `AbstractIoHandler`, `CompositeIoFilterChainBuilder`, `EventHandlingStrategy` (with `SingleThreadedEventHandlingStrategy` and `ThreadPerSessionEventHandlingStrategy` for single-vs-thread-per-session message dispatch), `IoSessionResponder`, `NetworkingOptions`, `ProtocolFactory`, `QueueTracker`/`QueueTrackers`/`WatermarkTracker` (backpressure/queue management), `SessionConnector`, `CriticalProtocolCodecException`.
- `mina/acceptor`: `AbstractSocketAcceptor`, `AcceptorIoHandler`, `AcceptorSessionProvider`, **`DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider`** (dynamic session creation on incoming logon).
- `mina/initiator`: `AbstractSocketInitiator`, `InitiatorIoHandler`, **`InitiatorProxyIoHandler`** (proxy support), `IoSessionInitiator` (manages reconnect interval array + multiple connect hosts/ports), `ConnectException`.
- `mina/message`: `FIXMessageDecoder`, `FIXMessageEncoder`, `FIXProtocolCodecFactory`, `PatternMatcher`.
- `mina/ssl`: `AcceptorSslFilter`, `InitiatorSslFilter`, `SSLConfig`, `SSLContextFactory`, `SSLSupport`, `X509TrustManagerWrapper`.

### 5. Message Store Layer
`MessageStore` interface + factories: `MemoryStore`, `FileStore` (+ `FileStoreSync`, `FileStoreMaxCachedMsgs`), `CachedFileStore` (in-memory cache over file), `JdbcStore` (DB-backed, with `JdbcUtil`, `JdbcSetting`, configurable table names + connection pool), `SleepycatStore` (Berkeley DB / Oracle JE), `NoopStore` (stateless/no persistence). Plus `MessageQueue` abstraction with `InMemoryMessageQueue` and `BoundInMemoryMessageQueue` (bounded queue for backpressure) and their factories.

### 6. Log Layer
`Log` interface + `AbstractLog` base. Implementations: `ScreenLog` (stdout/stderr), `FileLog` (separate event/incoming/outgoing files), `JdbcLog` (DB tables, configurable), `SLF4JLog` (delegates to SLF4J with per-category routing: event/error/incoming/outgoing categories), `CompositeLog` (multiplex to multiple logs). Plus `LocationAwareLogFactory`, `CompositeLogFactory`, `LogUtil`.

### 7. Dictionary / Message Model
- `DataDictionary` (in base): parses FIX XML dictionaries, validates fields/groups/components, msg-type/tag lookups, field-type checking, required-field validation, custom/UDF support.
- `Message` (base): FIX message with header/body/trailer `FieldMap`s, group iteration, parsing/serialization, header field handling, reverse routing.
- `Field`/`FieldMap`/`Group`/`MessageComponent`: typed field hierarchy, ordered field storage, repeating groups, components.
- `MessageCracker`: visitor base class auto-generating `onMessage(...)` overloads per typed message (used by Application callbacks).
- `MessageFactory`/`DefaultMessageFactory`: creates typed messages by BeginString + MsgType, version-aware.
- `DataDictionaryProvider`/`DefaultDataDictionaryProvider`: per-transport/per-app dictionary selection (needed for FIXT 1.1 session/app split).
- `ValidationSettings`, `MessageUtils`, `MessageSessionUtils`.

### 8. Codegen / Typed Messages / Versions
- `quickfixj-dictgenerator` module: `Generator`, `Message`, `Field`, `Component`, `ComponentComponent`, `ComponentField`, `Enum`, `MsgType`, `MsgTypeField`, `MsgTypeComponent`, `Repository` — generates typed Field/Component/Message Java classes from FIX XML dictionaries.
- `quickfixj-codegenerator` Maven plugin (and Gradle usage documented in `customising-quickfixj.md`) — `MessageCodeGenerator` with `Task` (dictFile, messagePackage, fieldPackage, outputBaseDirectory).
- Supported FIX versions (each a `quickfixj-messages-*` module): **FIX 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.0sp1, 5.0sp2, FIX Latest, FIXT 1.1**, plus `quickfixj-messages-all` (aggregate).
- FIX 4.0–5.0sp2 generated from QuickFIX XML dictionaries; **FIX Latest generated from a FIX Orchestra repository** via `quickfixj-orchestration` module. `generator.decimal` build property toggles `BigDecimal` vs `double` for Qty/decimal fields. Custom Rules-of-Engagement supported by editing dictionaries/orchestration.

### 9. Acceptance Test Definitions
Located at `quickfixj-core/src/test/resources/quickfix/test/acceptance/definitions/` — **481 `.def` scenario files**, organized by version subdirs (`fix40`, `fix41`, `fix42`, `fix43`, `fix44`, `fix50`, `fixLatest`, plus `future/server/fix43`). Scenario categories: `server/` (core protocol: logon/logout, seqnum gaps, resend, reject, garbled msgs, comp-id checks, test requests, session reset — e.g. `1a_ValidLogon`, `2b_MsgSeqNumTooHigh`, `14a_BadField`, `20_SimultaneousResendRequest`, `ReverseRoute`, `SessionReset`), `lastMsgSeqNumProcessed/`, `nextExpectedMsgSeqNum/`, `rejectGarbledMessages/`, `resendRequestChunkSize/` (chunked resend, gap-fill vs resend), `timestamps/` (higher-resolution timestamps), `validateChecksum/`. Java harness under `src/test/java/quickfix/test/acceptance/` (+ `resynch/`).

### 10. Config Keys (SessionSettings)
Config keys are NOT centralized — they live as `SETTING_*` string constants across classes. Comprehensive set found:
- **Session (Session.java)**: `HeartBtInt`, `CheckLatency`, `CheckCompID`, `MaxLatency`, `TestRequestDelayMultiplier`, `HeartBeatTimeoutMultiplier`, `NonStopSession`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `TimeZone`, `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `Weekdays`, `UseDataDictionary`, `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateUnorderedGroupFields`, `FirstFieldInGroupIsDelimiter`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `ValidateIncomingMessage`, `LogonTimeout`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTag`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ValidateSequenceNumbers`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetOnError`, `DisconnectOnError`, `TimeStampPrecision`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ResetOnLogon`, `Description`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `SendRedundantResendRequests`, `PersistMessages`, `ClosedResendInterval`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `DefaultApplVerID`, `DisableHeartBeatCheck`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`, `RejectGarbledMessage`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `RejectMessageOnUnhandledException`, `RequiresOrigSendingTime`, `ForceResendWhenCorruptedStore`, `AllowedRemoteAddresses`, `LogMessageWhenSessionNotFound`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `MaxScheduledWriteRequests`, `ValidateChecksum`, `AllowPosDup`.
- **SessionFactory**: `ConnectionType` (initiator/acceptor), `ContinueInitializationOnError`.
- **Acceptor**: `SocketAcceptProtocol`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `SocketAcceptAddress`, `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Initiator**: `ReconnectInterval`, `SocketConnectProtocol`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketConnectTimeout`, `SocketLocalHost`, `SocketLocalPort`, `ProxyType/ProxyVersion/ProxyHost/ProxyPort/ProxyUser/ProxyPassword/ProxyDomain/ProxyWorkstation`, `DynamicSession`.
- **NetworkingOptions**: `SocketKeepAlive`, `SocketOobInline`, `SocketReceiveBufferSize`, `SocketReuseAddress`, `SocketSendBufferSize`, `SocketLinger`, `SocketTcpNoDelay`, `SocketTrafficClass`, `SocketSynchronousWrites`, `SocketSynchronousWriteTimeout`.
- **FileStore**: `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `FileStoreMaxCachedMsgs`.
- **FileLog**: `FileLogPath`, `FileIncludeMilliseconds`, `FileIncludeTimeStampForMessages`, `FileLogHeartbeats`.
- **SLF4JLog**: `SLF4JLogEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogErrorEventCategory`, `SLF4JLogIncomingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogOutgoingMessageCategory`, `SLF4JLogPrependSessionID`, `SLF4JLogHeartbeats`.
- **JdbcSetting**: `JdbcDriver`, `JdbcURL`, `JdbcUser`, `JdbcPassword`, `JdbcStoreMessagesTableName`, `JdbcStoreSessionsTableName`, `JdbcDataSourceName`, `JndiContextFactory`, `JndiProviderURL`, `JdbcLogHeartBeats`, `JdbcLogOutgoingTable`, `JdbcLogIncomingTable`, `JdbcLogEventTable`, `JdbcSessionIdDefaultPropertyValue`, plus pool sizing (`JdbcMaxActiveConnection`, `JdbcMinIdleConnection`, `JdbcMaxConnectionLifeTime`, `JdbcConnectionTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionIdleTimeout`, `JdbcConnectionKeepaliveTime`, `JdbcConnectionTestQuery`).
- **SSL**: `SSLSupport.SETTING_USE_SSL` (`UseSSL`) plus keystore/truststore/cipher/protocol settings in `SSLSupport`/`SSLConfig` (exact key strings not fully enumerated — see gap below).

### 11. Examples (quickfixj-examples)
- **executor**: minimal acceptor — `Executor` + `Application` + `MarketDataProvider`; auto-fills any incoming NewOrderSingle with an ExecutionReport (sells to any buy).
- **banzai**: Swing-GUI order-entry **initiator** client — `Banzai`/`BanzaiApplication` + UI (`BanzaiFrame`, `OrderEntryPanel`, `OrderTableModel`, `ExecutionTableModel`), supports New/Cancel/Replace, tracks orders/executions, `OrderSide`/`OrderType`/`OrderTIF` enums.
- **ordermatch**: a simple in-memory **matching engine** — `Application`, `Main`, `Market`, `Order`, `OrderMatcher`, `IdGenerator`; matches orders between multiple sessions.

### 12. SSL/TLS Support
Dedicated `mina/ssl` package: `SSLSupport` (config keys), `SSLConfig` (holds keystore/truststore/protocols/ciphers), `SSLContextFactory` (builds `SSLContext`), `InitiatorSslFilter` + `AcceptorSslFilter` (MINA SSL filters for each direction), `X509TrustManagerWrapper` (custom trust manager). Enabled per-session via `UseSSL=true`; acceptor groups sessions by SSL config + bind address in `AcceptorSocketDescriptor`.

### 13. Scheduling (Session Schedule)
- `SessionSchedule` interface: `isSessionTime()`, `isSameSession(time1, time2)`, `isNonStopSession()`.
- `DefaultSessionSchedule` supports **four modes**: non-stop (`NonStopSession=true`), daily (`StartTime`/`EndTime` only), weekly (`StartDay`/`EndDay` + times), and **multi-weekday** (`Weekdays=Sun,Mon,...` comma list parsed via `DayConverter`). Uses `TimeEndPoint`/`TimeInterval` inner classes with day-of-week + time + TimeZone. `DefaultSessionScheduleFactory` builds from settings. Settings: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `NonStopSession`.

### 14. Reconnect, Dynamic Sessions, Proxy
- **Reconnect**: `ReconnectInterval` accepts a comma-separated list → `int[]` of escalating intervals; `IoSessionInitiator` cycles through `reconnectIntervalInMillis[]` (last value reused) and through multiple `SocketConnectHost`/`SocketConnectPort` endpoints on failure. `SocketConnectTimeout` (default 60s) for connect timeout.
- **Dynamic sessions (acceptor)**: `DynamicAcceptorSessionProvider` + `AcceptorSessionProvider` create `Session` objects on demand for incoming logons not pre-defined; gated by `DynamicSession` setting and `AcceptorTemplate`.
- **Proxy support (initiator)**: full HTTP and SOCKS proxy via Apache MINA `proxy` package (`ProtocolFactory.createIoProxyConnector`). Supports SOCKS 4 / 4a / 5 and HTTP 1.0/1.1 with optional Basic auth (`Proxy-Authorization` header), NTLM (`ProxyDomain`/`ProxyWorkstation`). Settings: `ProxyType`, `ProxyVersion`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `ProxyDomain`, `ProxyWorkstation`. Wired through `InitiatorProxyIoHandler` → `IoSessionInitiator`.

### Minor gaps (non-blocking for parity comparison)

## QuickFIX/J Feature Inventory (for parity comparison)
